case_name: Microcystin removal from groundwater influenced by surface water intrusion for drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for microcystin-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipal wellfield near a riverbank has shown episodic microcystin detections during seasonal algal bloom periods, likely due to surface water influence on the aquifer. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach to protect drinking water quality while staying within conventional operating capability. Existing staff can manage routine chemical dosing, filtration, and standard disinfection, but the utility prefers to avoid highly energy-intensive or specialized systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve effective microcystin control for potable use and align with drinking water health-based guidance while maintaining stable routine operation.
secondary_goals: use established and publicly supported treatment methods
provide a robust barrier during seasonal contamination episodes

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorption performance
core_unit_functions.filtration: capture particulates and carbon fines and provide treated water polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety after toxin removal steps

applicability_rationale: the contaminant is predominantly dissolved microcystin, for which activated carbon is a well-established control method
groundwater has relatively low turbidity and moderate DOC, which supports practical carbon adsorption performance
this approach is compatible with conventional municipal operation and moderate cost constraints
activated carbon systems are widely cited for cyanotoxin response in drinking water treatment and can be implemented as a strong seasonal barrier
potential_risks: adsorption capacity can decline if DOC increases during intrusion events
breakthrough may occur if carbon dosing or media replacement is not monitored closely
preoxidation with chlorine should not be relied upon casually because cell lysis concerns are important when cyanobacterial cells are present upstream

core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize dissolved microcystins and reduce toxin concentration rapidly
core_unit_functions.biological_or_conventional_filtration: remove oxidized byproducts, particulates, and provide polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological stability in distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: ozone is a proven oxidant for many dissolved microcystins and can provide fast toxin destruction
the source water has low bromide concern implied by typical freshwater groundwater conditions, making oxidation more feasible
this train can be effective for seasonal events where a clear dissolved toxin barrier is desired
potential_risks: ozone systems are more complex than adsorption and may exceed the preferred operational simplicity for a small utility
oxidant dose control and contact time verification are necessary for reliable toxin destruction
if cyanobacterial cells are present before oxidation, poorly managed oxidation can release intracellular toxins

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: adsorb dissolved microcystins ahead of solids separation
core_unit_functions.coagulation: aggregate particles and PAC for downstream removal
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove flocculated solids and PAC carrying adsorbed toxin
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection after clarification and filtration

applicability_rationale: this is a conventional and familiar process route when PAC can be integrated into existing treatment
it is suitable for utilities that already operate coagulation and filtration or can add PAC seasonally
the train offers practical flexibility for episodic contamination rather than constant year-round operation
potential_risks: PAC performance depends on dose, contact time, and carbon type
sludge production increases and residuals handling becomes more important
if dissolved organic matter rises, required PAC dose may increase substantially

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong physical rejection of dissolved microcystins and other solutes
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore suitable finished water chemistry after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in the product water

applicability_rationale: membranes are technically capable of controlling dissolved cyanotoxins
this option may be considered if the utility later faces multiple dissolved contaminants requiring broad removal
potential_risks: high energy and membrane replacement costs are difficult to justify for a single contaminant case
concentrate management is incompatible with the stated residuals handling limitations
pretreatment requirements add complexity beyond conventional operation

core_unit_functions.chlorination_as_primary_toxin_control: attempt oxidation of microcystins using free chlorine
core_unit_functions.contact_tank: provide reaction time for disinfectant contact
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove any incidental solids and polish treated water

applicability_rationale: free chlorine can oxidize some dissolved microcystins under controlled conditions
the chemicals and equipment are familiar to conventional groundwater utilities
potential_risks: chlorination alone is generally a weaker and less preferred primary barrier than activated carbon or ozone for microcystin control
performance depends strongly on pH, dose, and contact time
if intact cyanobacterial cells are present before removal, oxidation may release intracellular toxin and worsen short-term dissolved toxin levels
disinfection byproduct formation must still be managed
